app.title: Math with Smiles
app.subtitle: Learn with the Ten Frames method

game.countingDesc: Use Ten Frames to learn counting from 0 to 10!
game.additionDesc: There are already {{num1}} dots. Add {{num2}} more!
game.additionHint: Tap empty cells to add dots!
game.subtractionDesc: There are {{num1}} dots. Remove {{num2}} of them!
game.subtractionHint: Tap on dots to remove them!
game.puzzleDesc: There are {{number}} dots. How many more to make 10?

feedback.keepTrying: Keep going, you can do it!

info.title: About Ten Frames
info.whatDesc: Ten Frames is a simple way to learn numbers: a frame with 10 cells, arranged in 2 rows of 5. Kids see numbers, they don't just hear them. The method is used in schools worldwide.
info.howHelp: How Do They Help Children Learn?
info.howHelpDesc: Kids learn to recognize how many dots there are without counting them one by one. They see groups: 3 on top and 2 on the bottom make 5. A full row is 5. A full frame is 10. These become anchors for fast mental math.
info.countingDesc: Kids tap cells to fill them one at a time. Each filled cell is one object counted. This teaches that each number word matches one object, and kids can see at a glance how many they have.
info.additionDesc: The first number shows in one color, the second in another. The child fills the cells and sees both parts and the total. For 3 + 4: three colored cells, add four more, see seven in all.
info.subtractionDesc: The frame starts full, and the child taps to remove dots. For 7 − 3: seven cells filled, take away three, four are left. Kids see the amount getting smaller right in front of them.
info.bondsDesc: Puzzle mode teaches the pairs that make 10. If 6 cells are filled, how many empty ones are left? Kids discover that 6 + 4 = 10, 7 + 3 = 10, and so on. These pairs help with fast mental math.
info.benefitsDesc:
• Visual understanding of numbers
• Fast quantity recognition
• Strong number sense
• Fluent mental math
• Less math anxiety, through play
• Natural anchors of 5 and 10
• Easy shift from concrete to abstract
info.tipsParentsDesc:
• Let the child explore freely — there's no wrong way to fill a ten frame!
• Ask questions like "How many more do you need to make 10?"
• Celebrate effort, not just correct answers
• Short daily sessions (5–10 minutes) are more effective than long ones
• Connect ten frames to real life: "We have 3 apples, how many more to fill our box of 10?"
info.researchDesc: Ten Frames are recommended by the National Council of Teachers of Mathematics (NCTM) as a key tool for developing number sense in early childhood. Research shows that children who use ten frames develop stronger mental math skills and a deeper understanding of place value. The method is part of the Common Core State Standards in the United States and is used in curricula worldwide, including Singapore Math and Australian National Curriculum.
